## 2.1 Vectors

A *vector* $v \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is an ordered list of $n$ real numbers:
$v = [v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n]$.
Vectors are how the model stores many related measurements as one object.
Once words become vectors, the model can add, scale, compare, and transform them with ordinary arithmetic.

::: {.callout-note}
*Math Minute — Real Number*

A real number is any ordinary number on the number line: $-2$, $0$, $0.5$, $\sqrt{2}$, $\pi$, and so on.
The notation $\mathbb{R}$ means "the set of all real numbers."
So $\mathbb{R}^n$ means "all vectors with $n$ real-number components."
:::

In Python, the book represents a vector as a list of floating-point numbers:

<!-- snippet: src/python/common.py#vector_type -->
```python
Vector = list[float]
```
<!-- /snippet -->

This simple representation is enough for the small implementations in the book.
Every token embedding is a vector (Chapter 4), and attention compares vectors to decide which tokens matter to each other (Chapter 6).

### 2.1.1 Vector Addition

When two vectors have the same length, we can add them by adding matching positions.
The first component adds to the first component, the second to the second, and so on:

$u + v = [u_1+v_1, u_2+v_2, \ldots]$

The Python version follows that sentence directly: walk through both vectors together and add each pair.

<!-- snippet: src/python/common.py#add_vectors -->
```python
def add_vectors(left: Vector, right: Vector) -> Vector:
    return [a + b for a, b in zip(left, right)]
```
<!-- /snippet -->

For example, take $u = [3, 4]$ and $v = [1, -2]$.
The first components give $3 + 1 = 4$, and the second components give $4 + (-2) = 2$.
So:

$u + v = [3+1, 4+(-2)] = [4, 2]$

### 2.1.2 Scalar Multiplication

A scalar is a single number.
Multiplying a vector by a scalar stretches or shrinks the whole vector without changing how many components it has:

$c\cdot v = [cv_1, cv_2, \ldots]$

In code, this means multiplying every value in the list by the same scalar.

<!-- snippet: src/python/common.py#scale_vector -->
```python
def scale_vector(vector: Vector, scalar: float) -> Vector:
    return [scalar * value for value in vector]
```
<!-- /snippet -->

For $u = [3, 4]$, multiplying by $2$ doubles both components:

$2 \cdot u = [6, 8]$

### 2.1.3 L2 Norm

The L2 norm is the length of a vector.
For a two-dimensional vector, this is the Pythagorean theorem; for longer vectors, the same idea extends across all components:

$\|v\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \ldots + v_n^2}$

The implementation squares every component, adds the squares, then takes the square root.

<!-- snippet: src/python/common.py#vector_norm -->
```python
def vector_norm(vector: Sequence[float]) -> float:
    return math.sqrt(sum(value * value for value in vector))
```
<!-- /snippet -->

For $u = [3, 4]$, the length is:

$\|u\| = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = 5$

### 2.1.4 Unit Vector

A unit vector keeps the direction of the original vector but scales its length to 1.
To get one, divide the vector by its own norm:

$\hat{v} = v / \|v\|$

The helper below combines the two earlier operations: compute the norm, then scale by its reciprocal.

<!-- snippet: src/python/common.py#unit_vector -->
```python
def unit_vector(vector: Vector) -> Vector:
    return scale_vector(vector, 1.0 / vector_norm(vector))
```
<!-- /snippet -->

For $u = [3, 4]$, the norm is $5$, so each component is divided by $5$:

$[3/5, 4/5] = [0.6, 0.8]$

![Vector addition and norms in 2D](images/ch02_vectors.png){#fig-ch02-vectors fig-alt="Two vectors u and v shown in 2D space with their sum and norm labelled."}
